items.md: # 41 MEASUREMENT OF EARTH ELECTRODE RESISTANCE
items.value: 41 MEASUREMENT OF EARTH ELECTRODE RESISTANCE

items.md: A number of different test methods may be used to measure a site's overall resistance which are based on a fall of potential (FOP) technique. There are situations, typically in urban areas, where the earth electrode resistance may not be directly measures practicably. Alternative methods of assessing the earth resistance may include computer simulations carried out by earthing specialists.
items.value: A number of different test methods may be used to measure a site's overall resistance which are based on a fall of potential (FOP) technique. There are situations, typically in urban areas, where the earth electrode resistance may not be directly measures practicably. Alternative methods of assessing the earth resistance may include computer simulations carried out by earthing specialists.

items.md: However, in general the following common test methods may be used:
items.value: However, in general the following common test methods may be used:

items.md:
a) *Fall of potential 61.8 percent test* — This test is suitable for simple earth electrode systems such as found in LV installations and in 11 kV distribution substations.
b) *Fall of potential 61.8 percent test — Test using a clamp on CT* — This test is similar to the standard 3 terminal FOP test and has the advantages that it may be used to measure the resistance of a system without disconnecting the earthing and can measure the resistance of an individual electrode (again without disconnecting).
c) *Fall of potential — Slope test* — This test is recommended for earth electrode networks covering large areas, such as grid and primary substations sites, but may be used elsewhere if required.
d) *Fall of potential — 90°/180° test* — This test is used in conjunction with the 61.8 percent or slope method to verify the results obtained.
items.value:
a) Fall of potential 61.8 percent test — This test is suitable for simple earth electrode systems such as found in LV installations and in 11 kV distribution substations.
b) Fall of potential 61.8 percent test — Test using a clamp on CT — This test is similar to the standard 3 terminal FOP test and has the advantages that it may be used to measure the resistance of a system without disconnecting the earthing and can measure the resistance of an individual electrode (again without disconnecting).
c) Fall of potential — Slope test — This test is recommended for earth electrode networks covering large areas, such as grid and primary substations sites, but may be used elsewhere if required.
d) Fall of potential — 90°/180° test — This test is used in conjunction with the 61.8 percent or slope method to verify the results obtained.

items.md: ## 41.1 Fall of Potential Method
items.value: 41.1 Fall of Potential Method

items.md: In this method two auxiliary earth electrodes, besides the test electrode, are placed at suitable distances from the test electrode (*see* Fig. 51). A measured current is passed between the electrode *A* to be tested and an auxiliary current electrode *C* and the potential difference between the electrode *A* and the auxiliary potential electrode *B* is measured.
items.value: In this method two auxiliary earth electrodes, besides the test electrode, are placed at suitable distances from the test electrode (see Fig. 51). A measured current is passed between the electrode A to be tested and an auxiliary current electrode C and the potential difference between the electrode A and the auxiliary potential electrode B is measured.

items.md: The resistance of the test electrode *A* is then given by:
items.value: The resistance of the test electrode A is then given by:

items.md: $$R = \frac{V}{I}$$
items.value: $$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

items.md:
where
$R$ = resistance of the test electrode in ohms,
$V$ = reading of the voltmeter in Volts, and
$I$ = reading of the ammeter in amperes.
items.value:
where
$R$ = resistance of the test electrode in ohms,
$V$ = reading of the voltmeter in Volts, and
$I$ = reading of the ammeter in amperes.

items.md: **41.1.1** If the test is made at power frequency, that is, 50 c/s, the resistance of the Voltmeter should be high compared to that of the auxiliary potential electrode *B* and in no case should be less than 20 000 ohms.
items.value: 41.1.1 If the test is made at power frequency, that is, 50 c/s, the resistance of the Voltmeter should be high compared to that of the auxiliary potential electrode B and in no case should be less than 20 000 ohms.

items.md: NOTE — In most cases, there will be stray currents flowing in the soil and unless some steps are taken to eliminate their effect, they may produce serious errors in the measured value. If the testing current is of the same frequency as the stray current, this elimination becomes very difficult and it is better to use an earth tester incorporating a hand-driven generator. These earth testers usually generate direct current, and have rotary current-reverser and synchronous rectifier mounted on the generator shaft so that alternating current is supplied to the test circuit and the resulting potentials are rectified for measurement by a direct reading moving-coil ohm-meter. The presence of stray currents in the soil is indicated by a wandering of the instrument pointer, but an increase or decrease of generator handle speed will cause this to disappear.

items.md: **41.1.6** *Fall of Potential 61.8 Percent Test*
items.value: 41.1.6 Fall of Potential 61.8 Percent Test

items.md: The 61.8 percent test is the recommended test procedure for small earth electrode systems where the electrical centre of the earth system is known, particularly ground mounted distribution substations and pole mounted transformers.
items.value: The 61.8 percent test is the recommended test procedure for small earth electrode systems where the electrical centre of the earth system is known, particularly ground mounted distribution substations and pole mounted transformers.

items.md: However, if the resistance of a particular electrode is required, a CT clamp tester must be used as any cables connected to the earth grid contributes to the connection with earth.
items.value: However, if the resistance of a particular electrode is required, a CT clamp tester must be used as any cables connected to the earth grid contributes to the connection with earth.

items.md: To obtain reliable readings the current probe should be in a straight line with the earth system under test.
items.value: To obtain reliable readings the current probe should be in a straight line with the earth system under test.

items.md: A 90° / 180° test should be carried out to check the results.
items.value: A 90° / 180° test should be carried out to check the results.

items.md: A 50 m separation between the earth grid and the probe may be used and is usually adequate for smaller earth systems and the resistance measured is equal to the true value when the voltage probe is positioned at 61.8 percent of the distance between the earth electrode and the current probe. If the earth electrode system is of medium size containing rods, then these distances should be increased (*see* Table 17 for guidance).
items.value: A 50 m separation between the earth grid and the probe may be used and is usually adequate for smaller earth systems and the resistance measured is equal to the true value when the voltage probe is positioned at 61.8 percent of the distance between the earth electrode and the current probe. If the earth electrode system is of medium size containing rods, then these distances should be increased (see Table 17 for guidance).

items.md: To confirm the accuracy of the first reading the voltage probe should be moved to the 50 percent and 70 percent positions and additional readings taken.
items.value: To confirm the accuracy of the first reading the voltage probe should be moved to the 50 percent and 70 percent positions and additional readings taken.

items.md: **41.1.7** *Testing the Resistance of Specific Electrodes Using a CT Clamp Tester*
items.value: 41.1.7 Testing the Resistance of Specific Electrodes Using a CT Clamp Tester

items.md: Certain test instruments that may be used incorporate a CT which clamps around the electrode to be tested. Figure 52 shows a 3-terminal test with CT clamp. The CT may be used to detect the test current returning via that specific electrode and allows the resistance of the electrode to be measured without disconnecting it from the rest of the earth system.

items.md: ### 41.1.8 Fall of Potential — Slope Method
items.value: 41.1.8 Fall of Potential — Slope Method

items.md: NOTE — A large earthing grid, such as underlies a substation or encircles a transmission tower, has a commensurately large electric field in the soil. Locating a current test probe beyond this influence can typically require several hundred feet, which might be quite impractical. Also the electrical centre of the earthing system cannot be known if the current probe is not far enough away, the plotted graph will show the rising resistance curve associated with the grid running directly into the curve from the test probe. The slope method permits an estimate of where the resistance of the electrode under test maximizes.

The slope method permits an estimate of where the resistance of the electrode under test maximizes.

items.md: ## 42 MEASUREMENT OF EARTH LOOP IMPEDANCE
items.value: 42 MEASUREMENT OF EARTH LOOP IMPEDANCE

items.md: The current, which will flow under earth fault conditions and will thus be available to operate the overload protection, depends upon the impedance of the earth return loop. This includes the line conductor, the potential electrode located at the same distance as the potential electrode. A reading is taken under this condition. The current electrode is then moved out in 30-m steps until the same reading is obtained for three consecutive locations. The current electrode is then left in the last foregoing position and the potential electrode is moved out in 30-m steps until three consecutive readings are obtained without a change in value. The last reading then corresponds to the true value of earth resistance.
items.value: The current, which will flow under earth fault conditions and will thus be available to operate the overload protection, depends upon the impedance of the earth return loop. This includes the line conductor, the potential electrode located at the same distance as the potential electrode. A reading is taken under this condition. The current electrode is then moved out in 30-m steps until the same reading is obtained for three consecutive locations. The current electrode is then left in the last foregoing position and the potential electrode is moved out in 30-m steps until three consecutive readings are obtained without a change in value. The last reading then corresponds to the true value of earth resistance.

items.md: ## 43 EARTHING REQUIREMENTS FOR INSTALLATIONS OF DATA PROCESSING EQUIPMENT
items.value: 43 EARTHING REQUIREMENTS FOR INSTALLATIONS OF DATA PROCESSING EQUIPMENT

items.md: ### 43.1 General
items.value: 43.1 General

items.md: This clause covers the special requirements for earth electrodes at consumer's premises, and substations and any parallel metallic return to the transformer neutral as well as the transformer winding. To test the overall earthing for any installation, depending for protection on the operation of overcurrent devices, for example, fuses, it is necessary to measure the impedance of this loop under practical fault conditions. After the supply has been connected, this shall be done by the use of an earth loop impedance tester. The neutral is used in place of the phase conductor for the purpose of the test. The open-circuit voltage of the loop tester should not exceed 32 V.
items.value: This clause covers the special requirements for earth electrodes at consumer's premises, and substations and any parallel metallic return to the transformer neutral as well as the transformer winding. To test the overall earthing for any installation, depending for protection on the operation of overcurrent devices, for example, fuses, it is necessary to measure the impedance of this loop under practical fault conditions. After the supply has been connected, this shall be done by the use of an earth loop impedance tester. The neutral is used in place of the phase conductor for the purpose of the test. The open-circuit voltage of the loop tester should not exceed 32 V.

items.value: 43.2.1 Data Processing Equipment

items.md: Electrically operated machine units that separately or assembled in systems, accumulate, process and store data. Acceptance and divulgence of data may or may not be electronic means.
items.value: Electrically operated machine units that separately or assembled in systems, accumulate, process and store data. Acceptance and divulgence of data may or may not be electronic means.

items.value: 43.2.2 Low Noise Earth

items.md: An earth connection in which the level of conducted interference from external sources does not produce an unacceptable incidence of malfunction in the data processing or similar equipment to which it is connected.

items.md: #### 43.2.4.1 High integrity earth connections
items.value: 43.2.4.1 High integrity earth connections

items.md: NOTE — The aim of the requirements detailed below is to provide high integrity earth connections by using robust or duplicate conductors in association with permanent connections or robust connectors.
items.value: NOTE — The aim of the requirements detailed below is to provide high integrity earth connections by using robust or duplicate conductors in association with permanent connections or robust connectors.

items.md: Protective conductors shall comply with the following:
items.value: Protective conductors shall comply with the following:

items.md: a) Where independent protective conductors are, there shall be one conductor with a cross-sectional area of not less than 10 mm<sup>2</sup> or two conductors with independent terminations, each having a cross-sectional area of not less than 4 mm<sup>2</sup>;
items.value: a) Where independent protective conductors are, there shall be one conductor with a cross-sectional area of not less than 10 mm<sup>2</sup> or two conductors with independent terminations, each having a cross-sectional area of not less than 4 mm<sup>2</sup>;

items.md: b) When incorporated in a multicore cable together with the supply conductors, the sum total cross-sectional area of all the conductors shall be not less than 10 mm<sup>2</sup> and the protective conductors shall comply with **14** to **23**;
items.value: b) When incorporated in a multicore cable together with the supply conductors, the sum total cross-sectional area of all the conductors shall be not less than 10 mm<sup>2</sup> and the protective conductors shall comply with 14 to 23;

items.md: c) Where the protective conductor is installed in, and connected in parallel with a metal conduit having electrical continuity according to relevant Indian Standard specification on conduits for electrical purposes, a conductor of not less than 2.5 mm<sup>2</sup> shall be used; and
items.value: c) Where the protective conductor is installed in, and connected in parallel with a metal conduit having electrical continuity according to relevant Indian Standard specification on conduits for electrical purposes, a conductor of not less than 2.5 mm<sup>2</sup> shall be used; and

items.md: d) Rigid and flexible metallic conduits, metallic ducting and metallic screens, and armouring which meet the requirements of **14** to **23**.
items.value: d) Rigid and flexible metallic conduits, metallic ducting and metallic screens, and armouring which meet the requirements of 14 to 23.

items.md: Each conductor specified in (a), (b), (c) and (d) shall meet the requirements of **14** to **23**.
items.value: Each conductor specified in (a), (b), (c) and (d) shall meet the requirements of 14 to 23.

items.md: #### 43.2.4.2 Earth integrity monitoring
items.value: 43.2.4.2 Earth integrity monitoring

items.md: A protective device shall be provided which will disconnect the equipment, in the event of a discontinuity occurring in the earth conductor, within the voltage/time limits prescribed by relevant standards.
items.value: A protective device shall be provided which will disconnect the equipment, in the event of a discontinuity occurring in the earth conductor, within the voltage/time limits prescribed by relevant standards.

items.md: The protective conductors shall comply with **14** to **23**.
items.value: The protective conductors shall comply with 14 to 23.

items.md: NOTE — The aim of the requirements detailed above is to monitor the continuity of the protective earth connection and provided means of automatic supply disconnection in case of failure.
items.value: NOTE — The aim of the requirements detailed above is to monitor the continuity of the protective earth connection and provided means of automatic supply disconnection in case of failure.

items.md: #### 43.2.4.3 Use of double wound transformer
items.value: 43.2.4.3 Use of double wound transformer

items.md: Equipment shall be connected to the supply via a double wound transformer of other units in which the input and output circuits are separated, such as motor-alternator sets (see **44**).
items.value: Equipment shall be connected to the supply via a double wound transformer of other units in which the input and output circuits are separated, such as motor-alternator sets (see 44).

items.md: The secondary circuit should preferably be connected as a TN-System but an IT-System may be used where required for the specific application.
items.value: The secondary circuit should preferably be connected as a TN-System but an IT-System may be used where required for the specific application.

items.md: NOTE — The aim of the requirements above is to localize the path of the leakage current, and minimize the possibility of a break in continuity in this path.
items.value: NOTE — The aim of the requirements above is to localize the path of the leakage current, and minimize the possibility of a break in continuity in this path.

items.md: #### 43.2.4.4 Additional requirements for TT- system
items.value: 43.2.4.4 Additional requirements for TT- system

items.md: The requirements below ensure that the leakage in normal operation of all equipment protected by one and the same protective device is less than half of that required to operate earth fault protective devices for the installation circuit.
items.value: The requirements below ensure that the leakage in normal operation of all equipment protected by one and the same protective device is less than half of that required to operate earth fault protective devices for the installation circuit.

items.md:
* a) The total leakage current $I\_1$ (in amperes), the resistance of the earth electrode $R\_A$ (in ohms) and the nominal operating residual current of the protective device $I\_{\Delta n}$ (in amperes) shall be related as follows:
  $$I\_1 + \frac{I\Delta\_n}{2} \leq \frac{U\_L}{2R\_A}$$
* b) If the requirements of (a) cannot be met, the requirements of **42.2.4.3** shall apply.

a) The total leakage current $I\_1$ (in amperes), the resistance of the earth electrode $R\_A$ (in ohms) and the nominal operating residual current of the protective device $I\_{\Delta n}$ (in amperes) shall be related as follows:
$$I\_1 + \frac{I\Delta\_n}{2} \leq \frac{U\_L}{2R\_A}$$

items.md: ### 43.3 Additional Requirements for IT-Systems
items.value: 43.3 Additional Requirements for IT-Systems

items.md: **43.3.1** It is preferred that equipment with high leakage current is not connected directly to IT -systems because of the difficulty of satisfying touch voltage requirements on a first fault.
items.value: 43.3.1 It is preferred that equipment with high leakage current is not connected directly to IT -systems because of the difficulty of satisfying touch voltage requirements on a first fault.

Where possible, the equipment is supplied by a TN-system derived from the mains supply by means of a double wound transformer.
items.value: Where possible, the equipment is supplied by a TN-system derived from the mains supply by means of a double wound transformer.

items.md: Where it is possible, the equipment may be connected directly to the IT-system. This may be facilitated by connecting all protective earth connections for equipment using the IT-system directly to the power system earth electrode.
items.value: Where it is possible, the equipment may be connected directly to the IT-system. This may be facilitated by connecting all protective earth connections for equipment using the IT-system directly to the power system earth electrode.

items.md: **43.3.2** Before making direct connection to an IT-system, installers shall ensure that equipment is suitable for connection to IT - systems according to the declaration of the manufacturer.
items.value: 43.3.2 Before making direct connection to an IT-system, installers shall ensure that equipment is suitable for connection to IT - systems according to the declaration of the manufacturer.

items.md: **43.4 Safety Requirement for Low Noise Earthing Connections**
items.value: 43.4 Safety Requirement for Low Noise Earthing Connections

items.md: NOTE — It may be found that the electrical noise levels on the protective earthing system of building installations cause an unacceptable incidence of malfunction on data processing equipment connected to it.
items.value: NOTE — It may be found that the electrical noise levels on the protective earthing system of building installations cause an unacceptable incidence of malfunction on data processing equipment connected to it.

items.md: **43.4.1** Whatever measures are taken to provide a low-noise earthing connection, it is required that exposed conductive parts of data processing shall be connected to the main earthing terminal.
items.value: 43.4.1 Whatever measures are taken to provide a low-noise earthing connection, it is required that exposed conductive parts of data processing shall be connected to the main earthing terminal.

items.md: NOTE — The use of separate earth electrodes for simultaneously accessible exposed conductive parts is not permitted.
items.value: NOTE — The use of separate earth electrodes for simultaneously accessible exposed conductive parts is not permitted.

items.md: This requirement shall also apply to metallic enclosures of Class II and Class III equipment, and to FELV circuits when these are earthed for functional reasons.
items.value: This requirement shall also apply to metallic enclosures of Class II and Class III equipment, and to FELV circuits when these are earthed for functional reasons.

items.md: Earth conductors, which serve functional purposes only, need not comply with **14** to **23**.
items.value: Earth conductors, which serve functional purposes only, need not comply with 14 to 23.

items.md: **43.4.2** *Other Special Methods*
items.value: 43.4.2 Other Special Methods

items.md: In extreme cases, if the safety requirements of **43.4.1** are fulfilled but electrical noise on the main earthing terminal of the installation cannot be reduced to an acceptable level, the installation has to be treated as a special case.
items.value: In extreme cases, if the safety requirements of 43.4.1 are fulfilled but electrical noise on the main earthing terminal of the installation cannot be reduced to an acceptable level, the installation has to be treated as a special case.

items.md: The earthing arrangement has to provide the same level of protection as is generally provided by these requirements and particular attention should be given to ensure that the arrangement:

**44.2 Method of Connecting Transformers Physically Separate from Units**
items.value: 44.2 Method of Connecting Transformers Physically Separate from Units

items.md: The neutral point for the secondary circuit shall be connected to earth at the transformer and the earth connections between the equipment and the transformer shall comply with the requirements of **43.2.4.1** or **43.2.4.2**.
items.value: The neutral point for the secondary circuit shall be connected to earth at the transformer and the earth connections between the equipment and the transformer shall comply with the requirements of 43.2.4.1 or 43.2.4.2.

items.md: Connections shall be as shown in Fig. 57.
items.value: Connections shall be as shown in Fig. 57.

items.md: The diagram shows a transformer with primary and secondary windings. The primary is connected to L1 and L2 or N. The secondary neutral point is connected to the PE (Protective Earth) conductor. Two separate load units are shown connected to the secondary circuit. Each unit contains a filter capacitance (C), a LOAD, and EXPOSED CONDUCTIVE PARTS. The exposed conductive parts of both units are connected back to the PE conductor.
items.value: The diagram shows a transformer with primary and secondary windings. The primary is connected to L1 and L2 or N. The secondary neutral point is connected to the PE (Protective Earth) conductor. Two separate load units are shown connected to the secondary circuit. Each unit contains a filter capacitance (C), a LOAD, and EXPOSED CONDUCTIVE PARTS. The exposed conductive parts of both units are connected back to the PE conductor.

items.md:
Single-phase system depicted for ease. System may be 3-phase.
Primary and secondary circuits must have means of control and protection. These are not shown.
*C* is the filter capacitance.
*L1* and *L2* or *N* are connections to the incoming supply and *PE* is the connection from accessible parts of the equipment to the main earthing terminal of the installation for both protective conductors of Class I equipment and functional earthing conductors of Class II equipment.

items.value: Review of Indian Standards

items.md: Amendments are issued to standards as the need arises on the basis of comments. Standards are also reviewed periodically; a standard along with amendments is reaffirmed when such review indicates that no changes are needed; if the review indicates that changes are needed, it is taken up for revision. Users of Indian Standards should ascertain that they are in possession of the latest amendments or edition by referring to the latest issue of 'BIS Catalogue' and 'Standards : Monthly Additions'.
